channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: As Vance Leads Iran Negotiations, Trump Creates Disruptions in His Path
item.description: Vice President JD Vance is in a politically precarious spot.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: How Vice President JD Vance handles the future of the negotiations will factor into Republicans’ performance in the midterm elections and his future as a potential successor to President Trump.

item.title: Iran Makes Moves to Assert Control Over the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: After Iran weaponized the waterway by making it too dangerous for businesses, experts say, the country is now looking to charge fees to vessels seeking to transit the vital water.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels anchored in the Strait of Hormuz, off the port city of Khasab on Oman’s northern Musandam Peninsula last month.

item.title: America’s Thirst for Gasoline May Not Recover After Iran War
item.description: People drove less and bought more-efficient cars when fuel prices surged, habits that could stick over the long term.
item.dc:creator: Lydia DePillis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Hannah Beier for The New York Times
item.media:description: When gas prices started rising, Judy Vassallo, 89, started taking the city bus, which is free for seniors.

item.title: Markets Recoil in Global Sell-Off Driven by Tech Stocks
item.description: Chipmakers led the way down in South Korea, where the main index crashed 10 percent. Stocks in Europe were lower and S&P 500 futures pointed to a sharp fall.
item.dc:creator: Daisuke Wakabayashi and Jason Karaian
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The shares of South Korea’s leading chip companies, crucial to the boon in artificial development, plunged on Monday.

item.title: Can Andy Burnham Succeed in a Disgruntled Britain Where Starmer Failed?
item.description: The likely successor to Prime Minister Keir Starmer will inherit the same challenges of economic stagnation and ascendant populism. Will a divided nation be prepared to give him time?
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Andy Burnham celebrating after his swearing-in in Parliament on Monday.

item.title: Brexit Has Cost the UK Growth, Analysts Say, in the Decade Since the Vote
item.description: Citing lower trade and investment, analysts broadly agree that Britain’s economy is smaller than it would have been if the country had stayed in the E.U.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ten years ago, British voters approved by a slim margin a proposal for the country to leave the European Union. The economic results, economists say, have not been glowing.

item.title: Those British Strawberries Are Being Picked by Central Asian Workers
item.description: Ten years after Brexit, most seasonal workers in Britain are from countries such as Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan. Without them, agricultural chiefs say, many farms would fail.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle and Aigerim Turgunbaeva
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Shukrat Djuraev at Homefield Farm, in Kent, England, this month.

item.title: Major Supreme Court Decisions Testing Trump’s Policies Remain
item.description: Over the next two weeks, the justices will release more than a dozen final opinions, including high-profile decisions on birthright citizenship, the Federal Reserve and transgender athletes.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:description: The justices will resolve a series of high-profile cases testing the Trump administration’s policies to expand presidential power and reshape the federal bureaucracy.

item.title: In New York City, Early Voting Saw Major Changes From 2025
item.description: Turnout was low and the electorate was much older than it was last year when the mayoral race between Mamdani and Cuomo fueled heavy early voting.
item.dc:creator: Christine Zhang and Nicholas Fandos

item.title: Looking Back on the U.S.A.’s Many Founders, 250 Years In
item.description: In the past 50 years, the way we tell the story of the Revolution has become dramatically more complex. Can it still inspire us all?
item.dc:creator: Jane Kamensky
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Universal History Archive/Universal Images Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The five-man drafting committee presenting the Declaration of Independence to Congress, in an early 19th-century painting by John Trumbull.

item.title: How Mark Rutte of NATO Manages an Unpredictable Trump
item.description: Secretary General Mark Rutte is headed to Washington. His style has at times frustrated the very European leaders who need him to hold the alliance together.
item.dc:creator: Mara Hvistendahl and Steven Erlanger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump and Mark Rutte, the NATO secretary general, at the World Economic Forum in January.

item.title: Trump on the Shabby Condition of the Reflecting Pool: Not My Fault
item.description: President Trump said the blooms of green algae and the peeling polyurethane had nothing to do with the rushed $16.4 million makeover he had ordered.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Maxine Joselow
item.media:description: Ongoing work on Sunday at the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool, which has been troubled for decades by leaks and algae blooms that turn its water green.

item.title: Delcy Rodríguez, Venezuela’s President, Struggles to Uphold Trump’s Narrative of Success
item.description: President Trump says Venezuela, under U.S. oversight, has “never made the money” it is making now. But new oil revenue isn’t helping ordinary Venezuelans, and anger seems to be mounting.
item.dc:creator: Anatoly Kurmanaev
item.media:description: Delcy Rodríguez, the interim president of Venezuela, during a meeting with American officials in Caracas in April.

item.title: The Dispute Over Events for America’s 250th Birthday, Explained
item.description: Two national groups, including one backed by President Trump, have planned an array of activities to celebrate the country’s semiquincentennial. States are also hosting many events.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra and Jennifer Schuessler
item.media:description: A worker climbs a Ferris wheel being constructed for Freedom 250’s Great American State Fair, which will begin later this week.

item.title: China Takes Supercomputer Crown From U.S. For First Time Since 2017
item.description: A supercomputer in Shenzhen was declared the world’s fastest. It uses only standard microprocessors and not the special-purpose chips called graphics processing units.
item.dc:creator: Don Clark
item.media:description: China’s National Supercomputing Center in Wuxi in 2020. LineShine, a supercomputer in Shenzhen, has been declared the world’s fastest.

item.title: This Megafactory Is a Test Case for the U.S.-China Tech Race
item.description: Chinese firms have some of the world’s most advanced technology. But U.S. officials say relying on it could come with a downside.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Gilles Sabrié for The New York Times

item.title: Europe Created Heat-Wave Protections. Now Comes the ‘Crash Test.’
item.description: Searing temperatures in Western Europe are drawing comparisons to 2003, when a deadly heat wave sparked a reckoning.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.media:description: A visitor to the Palace of Versailles in France on Monday, where temperatures exceeded 104 degrees Fahrenheit.

item.title: What to Know About Heat in Europe This Week
item.description: Big parts of Europe are under high-level warnings for extreme heat, with forecasters predicting “severe” weather impact.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar

item.title: What is the Heat Dome Causing Europe’s Record Temperatures?
item.description: Heat domes happen all over the world, and the one over Europe this week is locking in days of near-record highs across Britain, Spain and France.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar
item.media:description: A swimmer in Paris on Sunday tried to cool off amid Europe’s second major heat wave of the year.

item.title: Clive Davis Knew to Let Patti Smith Have Free Rein
item.description: For Patti Smith, the best guidance was something that Davis rarely granted: free rein.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Ron Galella, Ltd./Ron Galella Collection, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Patti Smith and Clive Davis in 1993.

item.title: Social Security Is Running Out of Money, Fast
item.description: To save the program, we need to eliminate the payroll tax cap.
item.dc:creator: Bernie Moreno and Elizabeth Warren

item.title: Is This the Year Florida Turns Blue?
item.description: David Jolly must persuade voters who have been increasingly hostile to Democrats that he’s a different kind of Democrat.
item.dc:creator: Michael Grunwald

item.title: Late Night Dunks on Trump for Hiring Greenwater Services
item.description: Jimmy Fallon poked fun at the name of the business under fire for turning the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool bright green: “Nailed it.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)
Last Week Tonight With John Oliver (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Dark Smoke in a Sunny Place: Neighbors of L.A. Fire Struggle for Breath
item.description: The plume from the stubborn blaze in a cold-storage facility has dissipated, but people in the Boyle Heights neighborhood say they are in a toxic miasma.
item.dc:creator: Orlando Mayorquín and Maia Spoto
item.media:description: The towering plume of smoke dominated the Los Angeles skyline.
